title: The New HP Stands Up to be Counted
author: Peter S. Kastner
source_file: 2000 The New HP Stands Up to be Counted PSK.pdf
abstract: This Aberdeen InSight by Peter S. Kastner, published November 15, 2002 — the same day Michael Capellas resigned as HP president — argues that the market's 11% HPQ selloff on Capellas' departure was an overreaction. Kastner asserts that despite upcoming disappointing quarterly results, the merged HP/Compaq entity has a stronger long-term trajectory than at merger announcement 14 months prior, and outlines the strategic pillars supporting a bullish HP outlook: StorageWorks, Zero Latency Enterprise (ZLE), Itanium, Bluestone middleware, OpenView management, iPaq, and printer dominance.
importance_rationale: Written by Aberdeen's leading IT infrastructure analyst (and PSK file-name author Peter Kastner) on the day of a major HP management crisis, this InSight provided rare independent bullish analysis against prevailing market pessimism about the HP-Compaq merger — at a moment when institutional investors were re-evaluating HP positions.
relevance_rationale: The specific HP strategic pillars analyzed (Itanium, Bluestone, ZLE) are now historically resolved, but the analytical framework for evaluating post-merger IT vendor viability remains applicable. HP's subsequent split into HPE and HP Inc. (2015) provides a long-arc outcome benchmark for the study's thesis.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's bullish long-term HP view was partially correct — HP did survive and execute the merger, and HPQ stock recovered from post-Capellas lows. However, Itanium ultimately failed commercially, HP's PC/printer business declined, and the company split in 2015. His dismissal of the 11% selloff as an overreaction proved correct short-term but the long-term merger value creation thesis was mixed.
